title: It's Time to End the Egregious and Barbaric Practice of State-Sanctioned Revenge: Why Executions Must Stop.
teaser: 190 people have been exonerated from death row since 1973. A peer-reviewed study estimates at least 4.1% of those executed are innocent. The racial bias is documented in Supreme Court records. The botched execution rate is one in thirty-three. The case against capital punishment is not philosophical. It is empirical, and it is overwhelming.
body:
One hundred and ninety people have been exonerated from death row in the United States since 1973. One hundred and ninety men and women who were scheduled to die at the hands of the state, proven innocent before the machinery of execution could finish its work. According to the Death Penalty Information Center, which has tracked every exoneration since capital punishment was reinstated, the number stood at 190 as of early 2024. It is almost certainly still climbing.

That number alone should end the debate. It does not. So let us walk through the full record, because the evidence against capital punishment is not merely philosophical or theological. It is empirical, peer-reviewed, documented by federal agencies, and endorsed by every allied democracy on earth. The death penalty is not justice. It is revenge, administered arbitrarily, applied along racial lines, botched with horrifying regularity, and retained by a nation that has otherwise left these practices in the past where they belong.

The Botched Execution Record

The word "botched" is clinical. What it describes is not. In 2014, Clayton Lockett of Oklahoma writhed on the execution table for 43 minutes after a poorly placed IV caused his veins to collapse. Witnesses reported that he tried to lift his head and speak. He was not pronounced dead until prison officials closed the blinds to shield the scene from witnesses. That same year, Joseph Wood of Arizona took approximately two hours to die after being administered 15 doses of a sedative and painkiller combination. Witnesses counted him gasping for air 660 times. In 2017, Kenneth Williams of Arkansas lurched violently against his restraints and made what witnesses described as convulsing movements before finally going still.

These are not anomalies. They are data points in a documented pattern. Legal scholar Austin Sarat and his colleagues at Amherst College analyzed 8,776 executions carried out in the United States between 1890 and 2010 and found that approximately 3 percent resulted in botched procedures, meaning one in thirty-three state executions caused prolonged, visible suffering. Their findings were published by Stanford University Press in Gruesome Spectacles: Botched Executions and America's Death Penalty (2014). The research is meticulous and available. No credible counter-study disputes the methodology.

A complementary investigation published in PLoS Medicine in 2007, authored by Teresa Zimmers and colleagues at Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine, examined the pharmacology of the three-drug lethal injection protocol used in most U.S. states and concluded that the combination carries "a substantial probability" of causing a painful death while masking outward signs of distress. The third drug in the sequence, a paralytic agent, prevents movement and visible expression, meaning a person experiencing intense pain may appear completely still to witnesses and executioners. The peer-reviewed study called explicitly for the protocol to be reconsidered. Most states have not reconsidered it. They have instead fought in court for the right to keep the drug source secret.

190 Exonerations. The Real Number of Innocent Dead Is Higher.

When 190 people are proven innocent after being sentenced to die, the question that follows is unavoidable: how many of the people the United States has already executed were also innocent?

The most rigorous statistical answer to that question comes from a landmark 2014 study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. Researchers Samuel Gross, Barbara O'Brien, Chen Hu, and Edward Kennedy applied systematic statistical modeling to U.S. death sentence data and estimated that at least 4.1 percent of defendants sentenced to death in the United States are factually innocent. Given that more than 1,550 people have been executed since capital punishment was reinstated in 1976, the authors' figure implies a minimum of approximately 63 innocent human beings put to death by the American government. That is a conservative floor, not a ceiling.

The Death Penalty Information Center maintains a separate documented list of executed individuals where compelling evidence of innocence has emerged after death. Among the cases that have received the most scholarly scrutiny: Carlos DeLuna, executed in Texas in 1989, whose case was examined in a 2012 investigation published in the Columbia Human Rights Law Review. Researchers identified a likely alternative perpetrator, documented eyewitness accounts that exculpated DeLuna, and found that the original police investigation was fundamentally flawed. The likely perpetrator, Carlos Hernandez, is known to have bragged repeatedly to acquaintances about committing the crime. He died in prison in 1999 on an unrelated charge.

Cameron Todd Willingham was executed in Texas in 2004 for allegedly setting a house fire that killed his three daughters. He was convicted on the basis of fire forensics testimony that was subsequently rejected by multiple independent experts, including a panel convened by the Texas Forensic Science Commission. A report by fire investigation expert Craig Beyler, commissioned by the Commission itself, concluded that "a finding of arson could not be sustained" and that the original investigators had relied on "folk mythology" rather than science. The state of Texas has declined to formally acknowledge his likely innocence. Governor Rick Perry blocked the Commission's inquiry nine days before its scheduled hearing on the case.

The Racial Architecture of State Execution

The most extensive empirical study of racial disparity in capital sentencing remains the work of legal scholar David Baldus, statistician Charles Pulaski, and economist George Woodworth, documented comprehensively in their 1990 publication Equal Justice and the Death Penalty (Northeastern University Press). Analyzing more than 2,000 murder cases in Georgia and controlling for more than 230 variables related to the severity of the crime, they found that defendants convicted of killing white victims were 4.3 times more likely to be sentenced to death than defendants convicted of killing Black victims. Black defendants whose victims were white faced the highest probability of receiving a death sentence of any group examined.

These findings were presented before the United States Supreme Court in McCleskey v. Kemp (1987). The Court accepted the statistical validity of the Baldus research. It ruled 5 to 4 that the evidence did not prove unconstitutional discrimination in McCleskey's specific individual case. Justice William Brennan, in dissent joined by Justices Marshall, Blackmun, and Stevens, wrote that the majority's reasoning "seems to suggest a fear of too much justice." Justice Brennan further noted that the decision meant defendants challenging racial bias in capital sentencing faced an essentially impossible evidentiary standard. Warren McCleskey was executed in 1991.

The pattern documented by Baldus has not dissolved in the decades since. As of 2024, Black Americans comprise approximately 41 percent of the U.S. death row population while representing roughly 13 percent of the general population, according to data published in the NAACP Legal Defense Fund's quarterly Death Row USA report. A 2011 study by Glenn Pierce and Michael Radelet published in the North Carolina Law Review examined capital sentencing in North Carolina over a 20-year period and found consistent, statistically significant evidence that the race of the victim influenced who received a death sentence, even after controlling for aggravating factors in the crime itself. The finding replicated Baldus in a different state, with different data, two decades later.

This is not an accident of demographics. It is the documented, reproducible output of a system in which nearly 90 percent of elected prosecutors are white, public defenders in capital cases are chronically underfunded, and racial composition of juries has been subject to documented manipulation. In Foster v. Chatman (2016), the United States Supreme Court found unanimously that Georgia prosecutors had struck Black jurors from a capital case based on race, with handwritten notes in prosecution files including the letter "B" next to the names of Black prospective jurors and a ranked list prepared in case the exclusions were challenged. The defendant had been on death row since 1987.

No Evidence of Deterrence: Decades of Research, One Conclusion

Proponents of capital punishment have long argued that it deters murder. The empirical literature has spent 50 years testing that claim. It has not confirmed it.

In 2012, the National Research Council, the research arm of the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, released a comprehensive review titled Deterrence and the Death Penalty. The expert panel, composed of leading economists, criminologists, and legal scholars, concluded unanimously that the studies used to support a deterrence effect were "fundamentally flawed" and "should not be used to inform judgments about the effect of the death penalty on homicide rates." This was not a close call or a divided finding. It was a scientific consensus review commissioned by the most authoritative scientific body in the United States.

John Donohue of Yale Law School and Justin Wolfers of the Wharton School published a detailed econometric analysis in the Stanford Law Review (Vol. 58, 2005) examining the data claimed to support deterrence and concluded that the results were "fragile," "sensitive to minor changes in model specification," and "provide no reliable statistical evidence that capital punishment reduces homicide rates." Meanwhile, data compiled annually through FBI Uniform Crime Reports has shown consistently that states without the death penalty have lower murder rates than states that retain it. In 2022, according to the Bureau of Justice Statistics, the murder rate in non-death-penalty states was approximately 30 percent lower than in death-penalty states. The deterrent effect the system is supposed to produce is, empirically, nowhere to be found.

The Company This Country Keeps

In 2023, Amnesty International documented state executions in the following countries: China (thousands, estimated), Iran (at least 853), Saudi Arabia (at least 172), Somalia (at least 13), the United States (24), Egypt, and Kuwait. These are the countries that executed people in significant numbers. Every member of the European Union has abolished the death penalty as a condition of membership. Canada abolished it in 1976. Australia in 1984. South Africa in its post-apartheid constitution in 1995, explicitly identifying capital punishment as incompatible with human dignity. The United Kingdom in 1998. Nearly every democratic peer of the United States has concluded that this practice is indefensible.

The United Nations General Assembly passed Resolution 62/149 in 2007 calling for a global moratorium on the use of the death penalty, with 104 nations voting in favor. The United States voted against the resolution. As of 2024, 112 countries have abolished capital punishment in law or practice. The company a government keeps in its coercive practices reveals something about the values those practices express.

The Cost Argument, for Those Who Require It

For those unconvinced by the innocence record, racial bias, botched executions, deterrence failure, and international isolation, a fiscal case is available. The California Commission on the Fair Administration of Justice, a body established by the California Legislature and composed of judges, prosecutors, defense attorneys, and law enforcement officials, published a comprehensive cost analysis in 2008 and found that California's death penalty system cost $137 million per year more than a system imposing life imprisonment without the possibility of parole. The Kansas Judicial Council conducted a comparable analysis in 2014 and found that capital cases cost on average 70 percent more than comparable non-capital cases, primarily due to extended litigation timelines, mandatory appellate review, and the operational costs of death row housing. The death penalty is not efficient justice. It is expensive revenge that kills innocent people at a statistically documented rate.

The Verdict the Data Already Returned

The Supreme Court has already recognized that the Eighth Amendment's prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment evolves with the standards of a maturing society. In Atkins v. Virginia (2002), the Court ruled that executing people with intellectual disabilities violates the Constitution. In Roper v. Simmons (2005), it ruled the same for individuals who committed crimes as juveniles. In both cases, the majority cited evolving standards of decency and the near-universal consensus of peer nations. The logical extension of that reasoning to the practice itself has not yet been made. The evidence to support it has been available for years.

One hundred and ninety exonerations. A 4.1 percent estimated innocent-execution rate documented in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. A one-in-thirty-three botched execution rate documented by Amherst researchers and published by Stanford University Press. Black defendants sentenced to death at rates 4.3 times higher than white defendants when the victim is white, confirmed in Supreme Court proceedings. No credible evidence of deterrence, per the National Academies. More expensive than life imprisonment, per legislative commissions in multiple states. Condemned by 112 nations and the United Nations General Assembly.

The American death penalty is not justice. It is the state doing what it forbids individuals to do, doing it inaccurately, doing it along racial lines, doing it painfully, and doing it in the company of authoritarian governments rather than democratic allies. The primary source on this question is the evidence. The evidence reached its verdict a long time ago.
verifiedSource: Death Penalty Information Center (2024); Gross et al., Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (2014); Sarat et al., Gruesome Spectacles, Stanford University Press (2014); Zimmers et al., PLoS Medicine (2007); Baldus, Pulaski & Woodworth, Equal Justice and the Death Penalty, Northeastern University Press (1990); McCleskey v. Kemp, 481 U.S. 279 (1987); National Research Council, Deterrence and the Death Penalty, National Academies Press (2012); Donohue & Wolfers, Stanford Law Review Vol. 58 (2005); Pierce & Radelet, North Carolina Law Review (2011); NAACP Legal Defense Fund, Death Row USA (2024); FBI Uniform Crime Reports; Bureau of Justice Statistics; Atkins v. Virginia, 536 U.S. 304 (2002); Roper v. Simmons, 543 U.S. 551 (2005); Foster v. Chatman, 578 U.S. 488 (2016); California Commission on the Fair Administration of Justice (2008); Kansas Judicial Council (2014); Amnesty International Global Report on Death Sentences and Executions (2023); UN General Assembly Resolution 62/149 (2007); Columbia Human Rights Law Review (2012)
